title: Indications from Cracks
narrationScript: Every crack indication is a report on where the crack came from. Read three things — location on the part, orientation, and shape — and the crack usually files itself into one of three life stages. Cracks born during solidification tear hot, weak metal under restraint: ragged, branched hot tears at the thick-to-thin junctions of castings, straight centerline cracks down deep narrow weld beads, and small radial star cracks in weld craters. Cracks born during processing carry the process signature: quench cracks run deep and ragged out of keyways, holes, and sharp corners right after heat treat, while abusive grinding leaves fine, tight, shallow checks at right angles to the grinding direction. Cracks born in service are the quiet ones: a fatigue crack grows as a single sharp line perpendicular to the cyclic stress at a stress raiser, and stress-corrosion cracking branches into spider-like colonies under sustained stress in the wrong environment. The tightest cracks give the faintest lines — verify every faint linear indication before you let it go.

sections.heading: Reading a Crack Indication
sections.body:
A crack is a fracture — metal actually torn apart — and where it breaks the surface it traps penetrant along its whole length, so it shows as a **linear indication**. Most acceptance criteria call an indication linear when its length is at least three times its width. A Level II, though, reads more than the shape class. The indication is a report on the flaw that produced it:

- **Bleed-out strength.** The penetrant that returns is roughly proportional to the volume of the entrapment. A wide, strong, fast-growing line points to a deep, open crack. A thin, faint line points to a tight or shallow crack — or one partly plugged with soil or corrosion product.
- **Continuity.** A continuous line means the crack breaks the surface along its full length. A broken, intermittent line often means the crack surfaces only intermittently — or that peening, machining smear, or wire brushing sealed parts of it.
- **Shape.** A clean single line, a ragged branched cluster, a radial star, a fine network — each is a signature.

Interpretation then comes down to three questions: **where** is the indication on the part, **which way** does it run, and **what shape** is it? The answers usually place the crack in one of three life stages — solidification, processing, or service — and that is exactly how this lesson is organized.

sections.heading: Cracks Occurring During Solidification
sections.body:
Freezing metal passes through a weak, mushy stage — partly solid grains separated by films of remaining liquid. If the mold or the surrounding structure restrains contraction at that moment, the metal does not stretch. It **tears**.

- **Hot tears** in castings form at the last places to freeze under restraint: junctions of thick and thin sections, internal corners, and hot spots near gates and risers. Because the tear follows the weak grain boundaries, the indication is **ragged, branched, and often a cluster of short jagged segments**. Hot tears are relatively open to the surface, so they usually bleed strongly.
- **Surface shrinkage cracks** come from the same shortage — solidification shrinkage with no liquid feed metal left — and they keep company with hot tears at heavy sections.

A weld is a casting in miniature, and it cracks the same way while it freezes:

- **Centerline (hot) cracks** run straight down the longitudinal axis of the bead, where the two solidification fronts meet and low-melting segregates freeze last. Deep, narrow beads are the classic producers.
- **Crater cracks** form where the arc was broken abruptly: the unfilled crater solidifies and shrinks with nothing feeding it, leaving a small **star of radial cracks** — sometimes a single short line — right at the stop point. Small as they are, they are favored starting points for larger cracks in service.

sections.caption: Solidification cracks form at predictable places: ragged hot tears at restrained thick-to-thin junctions, centerline cracks where the solidification fronts of a deep narrow bead meet, and radial crater cracks at abrupt weld stops.

sections.heading: Cracks Occurring During Processing
sections.body:
Once the part is solid, the operations that shape, harden, and finish it can crack it — each in its own recognizable way.

- **Quench cracks.** Quenching sets up violent thermal gradients, and in hardenable steels the transformation to martensite adds a volume change on top. The combined stresses let go at **geometric stress raisers**: sharp internal corners, keyways, holes, threads, abrupt section changes, even stamp marks. The crack is typically **deep, open, and ragged**, often arcing away from the corner or hole that started it, and it bleeds heavily and fast. Timing is the tell — it appears at or soon after heat treatment, which is why specifications schedule penetrant testing **after** heat treat, not before.
- **Grinding cracks (checks).** A dull or glazed wheel, too much infeed, or inadequate coolant overheats a thin skin of a hardened surface. The skin cracks as a set of **fine, tight, shallow lines at right angles to the grinding direction** — in severe cases a complete network, called crazing. They trap very little penetrant and give the faintest indications of any crack family.
- **Hydrogen cracks from pickling and plating.** Acid cleaning and electroplating charge hydrogen into high-strength steel; under residual stress it opens fine cracks, sometimes hours later. The post-plating bake exists to prevent exactly this.
- **Delayed weld (cold) cracks.** Hydrogen cracking at the toe of a weld can appear hours or days after welding — the reason some welding codes deliberately hold the final inspection of crack-sensitive steels.

sections.caption: Processing cracks carry the process signature: a quench crack runs deep and ragged out of a stress raiser such as a keyway corner, while abusive grinding leaves fine, faint checks at right angles to the grinding direction.

sections.heading: Cracks Occurring During Service
sections.body:
Service cracks are the reason in-service inspection exists, and they are the hardest of all to see.

- **Fatigue cracks** grow under cyclic stress, usually well below the yield strength. They start at the surface, at a **stress raiser** — a fillet, hole, keyway, thread root, corrosion pit, tool mark, or any prior discontinuity — and grow **perpendicular to the principal cyclic tensile stress**. The indication is a **single, sharp, well-defined line**, short and faint when the crack is young. Fatigue cracks are the tightest you will inspect: at rest the faces can be nearly closed, and service soils can plug them. They demand scrupulous pre-cleaning, the full penetrant dwell, and an honest sensitivity level.
- **Stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** needs three things at once: sustained tensile stress (applied or residual), a specific aggressive environment, and a susceptible alloy — austenitic stainless steel with chlorides is the textbook pair. The signature is **branched, forked, spider-like colonies** of fine cracks. Corrosion product inside the crack can choke off penetrant entry, so even deep SCC may show weakly.
- **Thermal fatigue (heat checking)** covers heat-cycled faces — brake surfaces, hot-work die faces, exhaust hardware — with a **shallow craze network**. It resembles grinding checks; the discriminators are the part's heat-cycling history and a pattern that ignores the grinding direction.
- **Creep cracking** appears late in the life of high-temperature plant: grain-boundary damage that links up into surface cracks, typically at welds and section changes.

sections.caption: Service crack signatures: a fatigue crack is a single sharp line at a stress raiser, perpendicular to the cyclic stress; SCC branches into spider-like colonies; thermal fatigue crazes heat-cycled faces.
Crack-origin pattern guide — read location, orientation, and shape together. Disposition always follows the written acceptance criteria.
rows: Thick-to-thin junctions, internal corners, hot spots near gates and risers
Ragged, branched cluster of jagged segments; bleeds strongly
Longitudinal axis of deep, narrow weld beads
Straight continuous line down the bead centerline
Small star of radial lines (or one short line) in the crater
Keyways, sharp corners, holes, threads, stamp marks, section changes
Deep, open, ragged line; heavy, fast bleed-out
Fine, tight, shallow set at right angles to grind marks; severe cases craze into a network
Fillets, holes, thread roots, pits — any stress raiser, on parts with service cycles
Single sharp line perpendicular to the cyclic stress; faint when tight
Stressed regions exposed to a specific environment, susceptible alloys
Branched, forked, spider-like colonies of fine lines
Heat-cycled faces: brakes, hot-work dies, exhaust hardware
Shallow craze network that ignores grinding direction

sections.title: Tight cracks are quiet cracks
sections.body: The cracks that bring down hardware — fatigue, grinding checks, SCC — are exactly the ones that trap the least penetrant and draw the faintest lines. So a faint or intermittent linear indication is never wiped away and forgotten. Verify it: wipe the bleed-out once with a solvent-dampened swab, re-apply a thin coat of developer, and watch. Penetrant returning from below proves a surface-breaking flaw. If the surface was cleaned by a method that smears metal, re-clean and re-test before you believe a blank result. Virtually every acceptance standard rejects cracks outright, whatever their origin — your origin call is what guides corrective action and tells engineering where to look next.

figures.title: Solidification cracks: hot tears, centerline cracks, crater cracks
Processing cracks: quench cracks and grinding checks
Service cracks: fatigue, SCC, thermal fatigue

interactive.title: Crack Origin Classification
interactive.intro: Each card describes an indication pattern and where it was found. File it under the life stage that produced it — solidification, processing, or service. Let location, orientation, and shape do the telling.

items.label: Ragged, branched cluster of short indications where a heavy boss joins a thin casting wall
items.why: A hot tear — the junction froze last, and the mold restrained contraction while the metal was still partly liquid and weak along its grain boundaries.

items.label: Small star of radial indications at the stop point of a weld bead
items.why: A crater crack — the abruptly stopped crater solidified and shrank with no feed metal, cracking radially as it froze.

items.label: Straight, continuous indication down the longitudinal axis of a deep, narrow weld bead
items.why: Centerline hot cracking — the two solidification fronts meet at the centerline, where low-melting segregates freeze last and tear.

items.label: Deep, fast-bleeding ragged indication arcing out of a drilled hole, found right after quench
items.why: A quench crack — thermal gradients plus transformation stresses let go at geometric stress raisers like holes, and the timing right after heat treat is the clincher.

items.label: Set of fine, faint, parallel indications at right angles to the grind marks on a hardened bearing surface
items.why: Grinding cracks — localized overheating from abusive grinding checks the hardened skin perpendicular to the wheel's travel; tight and shallow, so the indications stay faint.

items.label: Fine cracking discovered on a high-strength steel part shortly after electroplating
items.why: Hydrogen cracking from processing — pickling and plating charge hydrogen into high-strength steel, which opens fine cracks under residual stress; the post-plating bake exists to prevent it.

items.label: One thin, sharp line at a fillet radius, perpendicular to the cyclic load, on a pump shaft with years of run time
items.why: A fatigue crack — it initiates at a stress raiser and grows perpendicular to the principal cyclic tensile stress as service cycles accumulate.

items.label: Branched, forked colony of fine indications on a stressed stainless steel part exposed to chlorides
items.why: Stress-corrosion cracking — sustained tensile stress, a specific environment, and a susceptible alloy together produce its spider-like signature in service.

checkQuestions.question: A casting shows a cluster of short, ragged, branched indications concentrated where a heavy boss joins a thin wall. What is the most likely origin?
options: Fatigue cracking from service vibration
Grinding cracks from final machining of the boss
Hot tearing during solidification — the junction froze last and tore while the metal was hot, weak, and restrained
Stress-corrosion cracking caused by mold-release chemicals
checkQuestions.explanation: Hot tears form at the last regions to freeze under restraint — thick-to-thin junctions, internal corners, hot spots. The ragged, branched cluster following weak grain boundaries is their signature; fatigue gives a single sharp line and grinding checks come as fine parallel sets on ground faces.

checkQuestions.question: Grinding cracks are among the easiest crack indications to miss. Which combination explains why?
options: They are very tight and shallow, so they trap little penetrant and produce fine, faint indications
They are entirely subsurface, so penetrant cannot enter them at all
They bleed so heavily that the developer film is overwhelmed
They occur only on soft, unhardened surfaces where penetrant spreads into background
checkQuestions.explanation: Grinding checks are surface-breaking but tight and shallow, so the entrapment volume — and therefore the bleed-out — is tiny. That is why they demand a high-sensitivity penetrant, full dwell, and careful viewing; they are not subsurface, and they form on hardened surfaces, not soft ones.

checkQuestions.question: Right after heat treatment, PT on a hardened steel shaft shows a deep, heavy-bleeding, ragged indication running out of the sharp corner of a keyway. The most likely cause is
options: a hot tear left over from the original casting
a fatigue crack from rotating-bending loads
thermal fatigue (heat checking) of the keyway face
a quench crack that initiated at the stress concentration during quenching
checkQuestions.explanation: Quench cracks start at geometric stress raisers — keyways, sharp corners, holes — driven by thermal gradients and the martensite transformation, and they are deep and open, so they bleed heavily. The timing immediately after heat treat rules out fatigue, which needs service cycles, and heat checking, which needs thermal cycling in service.

checkQuestions.question: A component with thousands of service cycles shows one thin, sharp linear indication at a fillet radius, oriented perpendicular to the principal cyclic stress. How should a Level II read this pattern?
options: Crater crack — typical of weld stop points
Fatigue crack — a single clean line at a stress raiser, normal to the cyclic stress, on a part with service time
Hot tear — fillet radii are where castings freeze last
Grinding crack network from original manufacture
checkQuestions.explanation: Fatigue initiates at surface stress raisers under cyclic load and grows perpendicular to the principal cyclic tensile stress — a single sharp line plus service history is the classic read. Crater cracks live at weld stops, hot tears at casting section junctions, and grinding checks come as fine parallel sets or networks, not one line.

checkQuestions.question: On an in-service austenitic stainless steel line operating under sustained stress in a chloride environment, you find branched, spider-like colonies of fine indications that are surprisingly faint. Why can SCC indications be weak even when the cracks are deep?
options: SCC cracks never exceed a few ten-thousandths of an inch in depth
Branched cracks are unable to hold penetrant by capillary action
Corrosion product inside the cracks restricts penetrant entry, so even a deep crack traps little penetrant
Chlorides on the surface chemically neutralize the developer
checkQuestions.explanation: SCC grows in a corrosive environment, so the crack is often packed with corrosion product that blocks capillary entry. Deep, dangerous cracks can therefore bleed weakly — which is why thorough pre-cleaning and full dwell matter, and why any opening treatment such as etching happens only with engineering authorization.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.3 (indications from cracks: solidification, processing, service)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (interpretation and evaluation context)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (indication interpretation guidance)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6, liquid penetrant examination (general practice context)
